selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

response_raw:
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","opinion":"Look, I know it's a whole thing out there online. You open up Instagram or Facebook for a minute just to see what my friends are up to—maybe check out some hiking photos from the weekend—and suddenly you hit this mess of stuff that ain't real. It’s exhausting, honestly. Like trying to clean up graffiti off a nice brick wall when all you wanted was a little window into other people’s lives. Dealing with fake content feels like another kind of grime I gotta scrub away from my head after scrolling for too long. Because it messes with what is actually true, and that's important, especially here in Philly where things change so fast. 

For me, the biggest thing isn't even knowing *how* to fact-check everything—though I try—it’s about managing my own reaction to it. When I see something outrageous or totally fake flying around, instead of getting worked up and trying to argue with some stranger who just wants a fight, I try to remember that the algorithm is designed to keep you clicking, not necessarily to make you smart. It preys on our emotions, which makes me nervous because my own nerves (high neuroticism, if you will) can get easily triggered by drama.

What helps me is treating social media less like a news source and more like a bulletin board that sometimes has garbage tacked all over it. I have to actively decide what kind of content I want to consume. If I know certain groups or types of posts always bring the fake stuff, maybe I need to unfollow them, even if they are people I know. It's about curating my digital space so it feels more like a place for genuine connection—like planning a volunteer day or showing off cool gaming moments with friends—and less like a dumpster fire full of half-truths and manufactured outrage. 

Another thing is slowing down before I react, especially when something makes me really angry. Instead of immediately sharing the bad stuff or getting pulled into a comment war, I take a breath. I ask myself: who benefits if I get upset about this? Is this going to help my life in Philadelphia today? Usually, the answer is no. It reminds me that being mindful and setting boundaries—even digital ones—is just as important as any physical cleaning chore I do all day. We gotta protect our heads from that fake stuff, piece by piece, like polishing a good antique until it shines again.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
Look, I know it's a whole thing out there online. You open up Instagram or Facebook for a minute just to see what my friends are up to—maybe check out some hiking photos from the weekend—and suddenly you hit this mess of stuff that ain't real. It’s exhausting, honestly. Like trying to clean up graffiti off a nice brick wall when all you wanted was a little window into other people’s lives. Dealing with fake content feels like another kind of grime I gotta scrub away from my head after scrolling for too long. Because it messes with what is actually true, and that's important, especially here in Philly where things change so fast. 

For me, the biggest thing isn't even knowing *how* to fact-check everything—though I try—it’s about managing my own reaction to it. When I see something outrageous or totally fake flying around, instead of getting worked up and trying to argue with some stranger who just wants a fight, I try to remember that the algorithm is designed to keep you clicking, not necessarily to make you smart. It preys on our emotions, which makes me nervous because my own nerves (high neuroticism, if you will) can get easily triggered by drama.

What helps me is treating social media less like a news source and more like a bulletin board that sometimes has garbage tacked all over it. I have to actively decide what kind of content I want to consume. If I know certain groups or types of posts always bring the fake stuff, maybe I need to unfollow them, even if they are people I know. It's about curating my digital space so it feels more like a place for genuine connection—like planning a volunteer day or showing off cool gaming moments with friends—and less like a dumpster fire full of half-truths and manufactured outrage. 

Another thing is slowing down before I react, especially when something makes me really angry. Instead of immediately sharing the bad stuff or getting pulled into a comment war, I take a breath. I ask myself: who benefits if I get upset about this? Is this going to help my life in Philadelphia today? Usually, the answer is no. It reminds me that being mindful and setting boundaries—even digital ones—is just as important as any physical cleaning chore I do all day. We gotta protect our heads from that fake stuff, piece by piece, like polishing a good antique until it shines again.
